title: Script P10: CRISPR Identification
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"b5rjd","text":"This protocol provides a method for identifing CRIPR repeats using the program PilerCR on assembled contigs. Based on the methods from the following publication: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cd3jg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8fb4l","text":"Hannigan, Geoffrey D., et al. \"The Human Skin Double-Stranded DNA Virome: Topographical and Temporal Diversity, Genetic Enrichment, and Dynamic Associations with the Host Microbiome.\" mBio 6.5 (2015): e01578-15.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":184,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"vrf7","text":"Perl scripts and other supplemental information available at:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1mlup","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9dn40","text":"https://figshare.com/articles/The_Human_Skin_dsDNA_Virome_Topographical_and_Temporal_Diversity_Genetic_Enrichment_and_Dynamic_Associations_with_the_Host_Microbiome/1281248","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":171}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"D0AF22835DF04E4E9532698631E4B1FC","url":"https://figshare.com/articles/The_Human_Skin_dsDNA_Virome_Topographical_and_Temporal_Diversity_Genetic_Enrichment_and_Dynamic_Associations_with_the_Host_Microbiome/1281248"}}}}
steps: In order to look at CRISPRs, we identified CRISPR repeats using the program PilerCR on our individually assembled contigs. First, we move to the correct working directory, copy over the contigs we want to work with and remove contig files that are empty. cd Raymkdir pilercr_contigscd ./ray_contigs_from_cat cd Ray mkdir pilercr_contigs cd ./ray_contigs_from_cat
Get the sample IDs for the specific samples in our cohort (16 subjects, 2 timepoints).

Get contigs. for file in $(cat skinmet_samples_to_include.txt); docp ./$file/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa Ray/pilercr_contigs/${file}_contigs.fadonecd Ray/pilercr_contigs/ for file in $(cat skinmet_samples_to_include.txt); docp ./$file/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa Ray/pilercr_contigs/${file}_contigs.fa done cd Ray/pilercr_contigs/
Remove empty contigs.fa files. find Ray/pilercr_contigs/ -empty -type f -delete

Identify crispr arrays with pilercr. PILER 1.06 Unix Edgar, R.C. for f in $(ls); doout=${f%contigs.fa}pilercr # the % sign indicates you are removing _contigs.fa and replacing it with pliercr (delete from end using the sortest possible match)out=${out##*/} # this cuts off the initial path from the file name, now output goes to the currently directory not the assembly directorypilercr1.06/pilercr -in $f -out $outdone for f in $(ls); doout=${f%contigs.fa}pilercr # the % sign indicates you are removing _contigs.fa and replacing it with pliercr (delete from end using the sortest possible match)out=${out##*/} # this cuts off the initial path from the file name, now output goes to the currently directory not the assembly directorypilercr1.06/pilercr -in $f -out $out done

Extract the crispr repeat sequences from the pilercr output. for file in *pilercr; dosed -n /d' | awk 'BEGIN{OFS="\n";} {print $NF}' > ${file/pilercr/repeats.fa} done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="81DC26CF972A470884CEF7004E1C29F8" > for file in *pilercr; dosed -n "/SUMMARY BY POSITION/,/Help on reading/p" $file | sed '/Array/d' | sed '/====/d' | sed '/SUMMARY/d' | sed '/Help/d' | sed '/^$/d' | sed '/>/d' | awk 'BEGIN{OFS="\n";} {print $NF}' > ${file/pilercr/repeats.fa} done
Concatenate all of the repeat sequences from all samples.

Since we do not know the direction of the CRISPRs, we write a function to identify the reverse complement of our sequences to use later on in our analysis. function rrev() {case $1 in function rrev() {case $1 in"T")echo -n A;;"A")echo -n T;;"G")echo -n C;;"C")echo -n G;;"N")echo -n N;;esac } function RC(){dna=$1dna=`echo $dna|rev`for i in $(seq 0 $((${#dna}-1)))dorrev ${dna:$i:1};done }

Once we have a list of all the repeats, we want to remove redundant repeat sequences and repeat sequences less than 20 nt long. Additionally, some repeat sequences can be substrings of other repeat sequences, with only one or two nucleotide differences on either end. To simplify our analyses, we only keep the shortest substring repeat. For instance, if one repeat was "AATCCGGA" and another repeat was "GAATCCGGAA", we only keep "AATCCGGA". Since we do not identify the direction of the CRISPRs, we remove repeat sequences that have a reverse complement to reduce redundancy. # Remove redundant repeats and repeats shorter than 20 ntcat crispr_repeats.txt | sort -u | awk -F, 'length($0)>=20' > crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.txt # Remove redundant repeats and repeats shorter than 20 nt cat crispr_repeats.txt | sort -u | awk -F, 'length($0)>=20' > crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.txt

Check to see if a repeat is almost the same as another repeat and only differs from another repeat by one or two nucleotides on either end if so, store it in nonunique_repeats.txt for line in $(cat crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.txt); docount=`egrep -c -w

If a repeat is a reverse complement of another repeat, add it to nonunique_repeats.txt. RCrepeat=`RC $line` RCcount=`grep -c -w > nonunique_repeats.txt fi else echo $name >> nonunique_repeats.txt fi fi done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="F8FCC01357F04AD696AC52D638FF3B1F" > RCrepeat=`RC $line` RCcount=`grep -c -w "$RCrepeat" crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.txt` if [ $RCcount -gt "0" ]; then if [ -f nonunique_repeats.txt ]; then RCcount_remove=`grep -c -w "$RCrepeat" nonunique_repeats.txt` if [ $RCcount_remove -eq "0" ]; then echo $line >> nonunique_repeats.txt fi else echo $name >> nonunique_repeats.txt fi fi done

If you have substrings in multiple repeat sequences, keep the shortest substring and remove the longer sequences. for line in $(cat nonunique_repeats.txt); doegrep > tmp_multiple_to_remove.txt done cat tmp_multiple_to_remove.txt RC_nonunique_repeats.txt | sort -u | sed '/^$/d' > tmp_to_remove.txt" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="B70E5AF7318B418E8B7E9010390D5E12" > for line in $(cat nonunique_repeats.txt); doegrep "$line" crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.txt | awk '{ print length, $0 }' | sort -n | tail -n +2 | awk '{ print $2 }' >> tmp_multiple_to_remove.txt done cat tmp_multiple_to_remove.txt RC_nonunique_repeats.txt | sort -u | sed '/^$/d' > tmp_to_remove.txt

Remove repeats that contain substrings of other repeats, add headers to the repeat files, assigning each repeat a number id. grep -v -x -f tmp_to_remove.txt crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.txt | awk ' {print

Pull out reads that contain the raw repeats. Because we do not know the direction of the repeats, we search for both the repeat sequence and its reverse complement in the contigs. cd Ray/pilercr_contigsmkdir raw_repeatscat crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.fa | while read line; doif [ " ]then # this line is a header so keep the name informationname=${line#>}else # this line is a repeat sequence; store the repeat and its reverse complementrepeat=$lineRCrepeat=`RC $repeat`path="Ray/pilercr_contigs"" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="1505F6F444DA4B979D037BE27DE2C7D6" > cd Ray/pilercr_contigs mkdir raw_repeats cat crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.fa | while read line; doif [ "${line:0:1}" == ">" ]then # this line is a header so keep the name informationname=${line#>}else # this line is a repeat sequence; store the repeat and its reverse complementrepeat=$lineRCrepeat=`RC $repeat`path="Ray/pilercr_contigs"

Search all of the contigs for the repeat and its reverse complement. If found, store the read where it is found is a file that is named first with what repeat it contains, then the sample ID it is found in. for samp in *_contigs.fa; docat $path/$samp | fgrep -B1 > ./raw_repeats/$name.${samp/_contigs.fa/}.reads.fastacat $path/$samp | fgrep -B1 "$RCrepeat" >> ./raw_repeats/$name.${samp/_contigs.fa/}.reads.fastadonefi done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="A098F722D9144872853309A25C0CBFA1" > for samp in *_contigs.fa; docat $path/$samp | fgrep -B1 "$repeat" >> ./raw_repeats/$name.${samp/_contigs.fa/}.reads.fastacat $path/$samp | fgrep -B1 "$RCrepeat" >> ./raw_repeats/$name.${samp/_contigs.fa/}.reads.fastadonefi done
Remove empty files. find ./raw_repeats -type f -empty -deletecd raw_repeats find ./raw_repeats -type f -empty -delete cd raw_repeats

What we are most interested in are the CRISPR spacers. Now that we have pulled out the samples containing repeat sequences, we need to pull out spacers from these samples. We are only interested in spacers that are flanked by two repeat sequences and spacers that are between 2 and 100 nucleotides long.

Loop through repeats and pull out spacers from each sample. for file in $(ls *.reads.fasta); do # determine which repeat we are working with from the file namename=${file/.MG*.reads.fasta/} # go back to the repeat fasta file and extract the repeat sequencerepeat=`grep -A1 -w
Replace all instances of the repeat in the file with the repeat name. For instance, if it is repeat 1, replace each instance of the repeat with ">>repeat1>>repeat1"
Replace all instances of the reverse complement repeat in the file with the RCrepeat name.

Format the output file. cat ${file/reads.fasta/tmp_to_keep.txt} | sort -u | sed '/--/d' > ${file/reads.fasta/tmp_to_keep_unique.txt}done cat ${file/reads.fasta/tmp_to_keep.txt} | sort -u | sed '/--/d' > ${file/reads.fasta/tmp_to_keep_unique.txt} done

Some samples have both spacers flanked by repeats and instances in which only one repeat sequence was found keep only the sequences that have flanking repeats remove *.reads.fata files that aren't used. find . -type f -empty -deletefor file in *.reads.fasta; dofile2=${file/.reads.fasta/_crispr_arrays.txt}if [ ! -f $file2 ]; thenrm $filefidonefind ./*tmp_to_keep_unique.txt -type f -empty -delete find . -type f -empty -delete for file in *.reads.fasta; dofile2=${file/.reads.fasta/_crispr_arrays.txt}if [ ! -f $file2 ]; thenrm $filefi done find ./*tmp_to_keep_unique.txt -type f -empty -delete

Extract only the sequences that have flanking repeats (we will blast them later). for file in $(ls *.tmp_to_keep_unique.txt ); dofor line in $( cat $file ); dogrep -w -A 1 > ${file/tmp_to_keep_unique.txt/reads.good.fasta}done done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="D01BEEF0188A4F55BD925B7F9BAD9834" > for file in $(ls *.tmp_to_keep_unique.txt ); dofor line in $( cat $file ); dogrep -w -A 1 "$line" ${file/tmp_to_keep_unique.txt/reads.fasta} >> ${file/tmp_to_keep_unique.txt/reads.good.fasta}done done

Now we need to do some file formatting. We have files named *_crispr_arrays.txt that contain repeat/spacer information. Each line contains the following information: >>repeat_number>>[spacer sequence]>>repeat_number

First, we combine all of the repeat/spacers into one file and keep only the unique sequnces and then we extract spacer sequences. # concatenate all arrays and remove repetitive arrayscat *_crispr_arrays.txt | sort -u > crispr_arrays_unique.txt # concatenate all arrays and remove repetitive arrays cat *_crispr_arrays.txt | sort -u > crispr_arrays_unique.txt

Extract spacers. Split the file so that each line contains either a repeat id or a spacer sequence. cat crispr_arrays_unique.txt | tr '>' '\n' | sed '/^$/d' > spacers_tmp.txtfor line in $(cat spacers_tmp.txt); do # if the line contains the repeat id, store it as the nameif [ cat crispr_arrays_unique.txt | tr '>' '\n' | sed '/^$/d' > spacers_tmp.txt for line in $(cat spacers_tmp.txt); do # if the line contains the repeat id, store it as the nameif [ "${line:0:2}" == "re" ]; thenname=$lineelse
Check to see if the repeat/spacer combination is a repeat. if [ -f spacers_tmp2.txt ]; then spacerRC=`RC $line`countSpacer=`grep -c -w

Format spacer headers so they are informative. spacer_count=1cat spacers_tmp2.txt| sed '/^$/d' | while read line; do echo spacer_"$spacer_count >> spacers.fa echo $line >> spacers.fa let spacer_count++ done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="C2D8604E7AD049C9B73C6BCCDFC99842" > spacer_count=1 cat spacers_tmp2.txt| sed '/^$/d' | while read line; do echo ">spacer_"$spacer_count >> spacers.fa echo $line >> spacers.fa let spacer_count++ done

Get length of spacers. for line in $(cat spacers.fa); doif [ " ]; thenname=`echo $line | sed 's/>//g'`elseecho $line | awk -v name=$name '{print name"\n"length($0)}' >> spacer_lengths.txtfi done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="2C6C8ACAB0A34B7D98FFCAEC433FE780" > for line in $(cat spacers.fa); doif [ "${line:0:1}" == ">" ]; thenname=`echo $line | sed 's/>//g'`elseecho $line | awk -v name=$name '{print name"\n"length($0)}' >> spacer_lengths.txtfi done
Since we have a concise list of unique spacers associated with their repeats, we can now blast viral contigs against the spacers. We keep hits with 97% identity.

Fix viral contigs for blast. for file in $(ls ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/); do#Remove block format in contig fasta file | Next three part of same thing | Replace the spaces in the contig names with underscores | Add sample ID to the end of each namesed -r 's/\s/_/g' ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file}/Contigs.fasta | sed 's/^\([A,T,G,C,n]*\)$/\1\@/g' | sed ':a;N;$!ba;s/\@\n\([A,C,G,T,n]\)/\1/g' | sed 's/\@//g' | sed '/\>/s/ /_/g' | sed /s/$/\_$file/" > ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="D407C4578A1E4B3D836AD4A6E356DC99" > for file in $(ls ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/); do#Remove block format in contig fasta file | Next three part of same thing | Replace the spaces in the contig names with underscores | Add sample ID to the end of each namesed -r 's/\s/_/g' ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file}/Contigs.fasta | sed 's/^\([A,T,G,C,n]*\)$/\1\@/g' | sed ':a;N;$!ba;s/\@\n\([A,C,G,T,n]\)/\1/g' | sed 's/\@//g' | sed '/\>/s/ /_/g' | sed "/>/s/$/\_$file/" > ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa done
Remove empty contig files. find ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/ -type f -empty -delete
Look at viral samples from our specific cohort (16 subjects, 2 timepoints).

Make a blast database for each sample from the sample contig file. for file in $(cat virome_samples_to_include.txt); domakeblastdb -in ray_virome_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa -dbtype nucl -out ray_virome_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file}donemkdir viral_contig_blastn for file in $(cat virome_samples_to_include.txt); domakeblastdb -in ray_virome_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file}_Contigs_with_format.fa -dbtype nucl -out ray_virome_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/${file} done mkdir viral_contig_blastn

Blast spacers against viral contig databases. BLAST Toolkit 2.2.0 Unix ftp://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/blast/executables/blast+/LATEST/ NCBI for file in $(ls ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/MG100*.nhr); do name=`echo $file | sed 's/^.*M/M/g' | sed 's/.nhr//g'` echo $name blastn -query spacers.fa -out ./viral_contig_blastn/${name}_blastn.txt -db ${file/.nhr/} -outfmt 6 -num_threads 32 -gapopen 5 -gapextend 2 -perc_identity 97done for file in $(ls ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/MG100*.nhr); do name=`echo $file | sed 's/^.*M/M/g' | sed 's/.nhr//g'` echo $name blastn -query spacers.fa -out ./viral_contig_blastn/${name}_blastn.txt -db ${file/.nhr/} -outfmt 6 -num_threads 32 -gapopen 5 -gapextend 2 -perc_identity 97 done

Once we have the blast hits, we only want to keep hits that are within 3 nucleotides of the same length of our spacers. for line in $(cat spacer_lengths.txt); do if [ = len {print $0}' >> ./viral_contig_blastn/${name}_hits.txt done fi done find ./viral_contig_blastn -type f -empty -delete" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="3356C8D6120D4ED49048D24AE653A2D1" > for line in $(cat spacer_lengths.txt); do if [ "${line:0:1}" == "s" ]; then name=${line} else length=`expr $line - 3` for file in ./viral_contig_blastn/*_blastn.txt; do grep -w "$name" $file | awk -v len="$length" '$4 >= len {print $0}' >> ./viral_contig_blastn/${name}_hits.txt done fi done find ./viral_contig_blastn -type f -empty -delete

We can format and combine our blast hits. for file in ./viral_contig_blastn/spacer*_hits.txt; docat $file | awk '{ print $1 > ./viral_contig_blastn/all_viral_hits.txt done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="532BE9F6ED664CF8A185E6CBBC4C5D20" > for file in ./viral_contig_blastn/spacer*_hits.txt; docat $file | awk '{ print $1"\t"$2 }' >> ./viral_contig_blastn/all_viral_hits.txt done

We want to know the identify of the viral sequences that contain CRISPR spacer hits. cat /project/egricelab/SkinMetVirome_tmp_files/ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/*_Contigs_with_format.fa > ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_contigs.facat ./viral_contig_blastn/all_viral_hits.txt | awk '{print $2}' | sort -u > ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_contig_hits.txtfor line in $(cat ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_contig_hits.txt ); dogrep -A 1 > ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs.fa done blastx -query ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs.fa -out ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn.txt -db references/UniProt-Virus-Phage/uniprot_virus_and_phage_TrEMBL_db -outfmt 7 -num_threads 32 -max_target_seqs 1 -evalue 1e-5 grep "^contig*" ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn.txt | awk '{print $1"\t"$2}' >> ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn_formatted.txt grep -B 2 "# 0 hits found" ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn.txt | sed 's/# Query: //g' | sed '/# Database/d' | sed '/# 0 hits found/d' | sed '/--/d' | awk '{print $1 "\tNot_Assigned"}' >> ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn_formatted.txt" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="4B159880FC1A467C920AF199D2661BF9" > cat /project/egricelab/SkinMetVirome_tmp_files/ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/*_Contigs_with_format.fa > ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_contigs.fa cat ./viral_contig_blastn/all_viral_hits.txt | awk '{print $2}' | sort -u > ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_contig_hits.txt for line in $(cat ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_contig_hits.txt ); dogrep -A 1 "$line" ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_contigs.fa >> ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs.fa done blastx -query ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs.fa -out ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn.txt -db references/UniProt-Virus-Phage/uniprot_virus_and_phage_TrEMBL_db -outfmt 7 -num_threads 32 -max_target_seqs 1 -evalue 1e-5 grep "^contig*" ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn.txt | awk '{print $1"\t"$2}' >> ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn_formatted.txt grep -B 2 "# 0 hits found" ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn.txt | sed 's/# Query: //g' | sed '/# Database/d' | sed '/# 0 hits found/d' | sed '/--/d' | awk '{print $1 "\tNot_Assigned"}' >> ./viral_contig_blastn/viral_hit_seqs_blastn_formatted.txt
We also want to know the identity of the bacterial host. mkdir read_assignment

Look at reads containing crispr arrays for each repeat. This generates a fasta file for each repeat. for file in *reads.good.fasta; docat $file >> ./read_assignment/${file/.MG*/}.fadonecd read_assignmentfind . -type f -empty -deletefor file in $(ls); do# blast these reads against the NCBI non redundant database, keeping the top hitblastn -query $file -out ${file/.fa/_blastn.txt} -db references/ncbi/nt -outfmt '7 qseqid staxids' -num_threads 32 -evalue 1e-10 -max_target_seqs 1 for file in *reads.good.fasta; docat $file >> ./read_assignment/${file/.MG*/}.fa done cd read_assignment find . -type f -empty -delete for file in $(ls); do # blast these reads against the NCBI non redundant database, keeping the top hitblastn -query $file -out ${file/.fa/_blastn.txt} -db references/ncbi/nt -outfmt '7 qseqid staxids' -num_threads 32 -evalue 1e-10 -max_target_seqs 1

Format the output so it is tab delimited with the contig id in the first column and taxonomic assignment in the second column. grep > ${file/.fa/_blastn_formatted.txt}grep -B 2 "# 0 hits found" ${file/.fa/_blastn.txt} | sed 's/# Query: //g' | sed '/# Database/d' | sed '/# 0 hits found/d' | sed '/--/d' | awk '{print $1 "\tNot_Assigned"}' >> ${file/.fa/_blastn_formatted.txt} done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="9F12BEC8ADE44D53BDFAEFB0730E0F11" > grep "^contig*" ${file/.fa/_blastn.txt} >> ${file/.fa/_blastn_formatted.txt}grep -B 2 "# 0 hits found" ${file/.fa/_blastn.txt} | sed 's/# Query: //g' | sed '/# Database/d' | sed '/# 0 hits found/d' | sed '/--/d' | awk '{print $1 "\tNot_Assigned"}' >> ${file/.fa/_blastn_formatted.txt} done
Remove empty files. find . -type f -empty -delete

Add a column to the formatted blast output containing the repeat information so the *_blastn_formatted_labeled.txt has 3 columns; crispr repeat, contig id, and taxonomic assignment. for file in *_blastn_formatted.txt; doawk -F, '{print FILENAME , $0}' OFS= ${file/.txt/_labeled.txt} done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="B42B00BB6F814279B7BA80BE296A45D1" > for file in *_blastn_formatted.txt; doawk -F, '{print FILENAME , $0}' OFS="\t" $file > ${file/.txt/_labeled.txt} done
Combine all blast hits.

Finally, we want to map each repeat, spacer combination to the metagenomic sample it is found in. for file in repeat*.fa; do name=${file/.fa/} repeat=`grep -A 1 -w repeat*/d'` RCrepeat=`RC $repeat` for line in $(cat ../spacers.fa); do if [ "${line:0:1}" == ">" ]; then spacerName=${line#>} elseegrep -B 1 "$repeat$line$repeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txtegrep -B 1 "$RCrepeat$line$RCrepeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txt spacer_RC=`RC $line` egrep -B 1 "$repeat$spacer_RC$repeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txt egrep -B 1 "$RCrepeat$spacer_RC$RCrepeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txt fi done done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="997D1DEC56B34C458DB281BC464CC5EB" > for file in repeat*.fa; do name=${file/.fa/} repeat=`grep -A 1 -w "$name" ../../crispr_repeats_unique_sorted.fa | sed '/^>repeat*/d'` RCrepeat=`RC $repeat` for line in $(cat ../spacers.fa); do if [ "${line:0:1}" == ">" ]; then spacerName=${line#>} elseegrep -B 1 "$repeat$line$repeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txtegrep -B 1 "$RCrepeat$line$RCrepeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txt spacer_RC=`RC $line` egrep -B 1 "$repeat$spacer_RC$repeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txt egrep -B 1 "$RCrepeat$spacer_RC$RCrepeat" $file | grep "^>" | sed 's/>//g' | awk -v name=$name -v spacer=$spacerName '{print name"_"spacer"\t"$0}' >> read_spacer_assignment.txt fi done done

The file read_spacer_assignment.txt contains two columns; the first with the repeat and spacer id and the second with the contig it is found in. We can now format files in R for easier analysis. To determine whether the CRISPRs were targeting coding or non-coding regions in phage, we blasted the CRISPR spacers against the Glimmer identified ORFs from the viral contigs that the spacers mapped to.
Keep only info about repeat, spacer, skinmet contig, viral contig, and viral sample body site.

Paste repeat and spacer together and format file. awk '{print $0 tmp2.txt head -n 1 tmp2.txt > formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt tail -n+2 tmp2.txt | sort -u >> formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt rm tmp2.txt" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="A56E025C3A0844F8BC7E6DF24C6F3AD9" > awk '{print $0"\t"$2"_"$1}' crispr_contig_hits.txt | cut -f 6,4 > tmp2.txt head -n 1 tmp2.txt > formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt tail -n+2 tmp2.txt | sort -u >> formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt rm tmp2.txt

Extract the Glimmer predicted open reading frames for the contigs targeted by CRISPR spacers. tail -n+2 formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt | awk '{print $1}' | while read LINE; doSAMPLE=${LINE/contig*nucleotides_/}grep -A 1 $LINE /project/egricelab/SkinMetVirome_tmp_files/ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/glimmer3/output/${SAMPLE}_no_block.genes > ${LINE}_orfs.fadone tail -n+2 formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt | awk '{print $1}' | while read LINE; doSAMPLE=${LINE/contig*nucleotides_/}grep -A 1 $LINE /project/egricelab/SkinMetVirome_tmp_files/ray_contigs_neg_clean_for_crisprs/glimmer3/output/${SAMPLE}_no_block.genes > ${LINE}_orfs.fa done
Remove empty files. find . -type f -empty -delete

BLAST spacers against the open reading frames in the virome they came from. for file in *_orfs.fa; domakeblastdb -dbtype nucl -in $file -out ${file/.fa/_db}donetail -n+2 formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt | while read LINE; doCONTIG=${LINE/repeat*/};CONTIG=`echo $CONTIG | sed 's/[ \t]*$//'`;SPACER=${LINE/contig*MG100[0-9][0-9][0-9]*repeat_*_spacer/spacer};grep -w -A 1 $SPACER ./raw_repeats/spacers.fa > tmp_seq.fa;if [ -f ${CONTIG}_orfs.fa ]; thenblastn -query tmp_seq.fa -out ${SPACER}_${CONTIG}_blast.txt -db ${CONTIG}_orfs_db -outfmt 7 -num_threads 32 -evalue 1e-10 -max_target_seqs 1 -perc_identity 97firm tmp_seq.fa;done for file in *_orfs.fa; domakeblastdb -dbtype nucl -in $file -out ${file/.fa/_db} done tail -n+2 formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt | while read LINE; doCONTIG=${LINE/repeat*/};CONTIG=`echo $CONTIG | sed 's/[ \t]*$//'`;SPACER=${LINE/contig*MG100[0-9][0-9][0-9]*repeat_*_spacer/spacer};grep -w -A 1 $SPACER ./raw_repeats/spacers.fa > tmp_seq.fa;if [ -f ${CONTIG}_orfs.fa ]; thenblastn -query tmp_seq.fa -out ${SPACER}_${CONTIG}_blast.txt -db ${CONTIG}_orfs_db -outfmt 7 -num_threads 32 -evalue 1e-10 -max_target_seqs 1 -perc_identity 97firm tmp_seq.fa; done

Find which spacers hit open reading frames and which ones do not. for file in spacer*blast.txt; docount=`grep -c > crispr_orf_hits.txt;fi; done" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="283FF2EE7D0F4C428C8F91594FAD5561" > for file in spacer*blast.txt; docount=`grep -c "# 0 hits found" $file`;if [ $count -gt 0 ]; thenrm $file;elsegrep "^spacer*" $file >> crispr_orf_hits.txt;fi; done

Count how many total hits there were and how many hits were to open reading frames. echo crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt tail -n+2 formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt | wc -l >> crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt echo "CRISPR HITS TO ORFS" >> crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt wc -l crispr_orf_hits.txt >> crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt" description="" os_version="" os_name="" guid="75514F79F57A4C01A7431199A7B5A742" > echo "CRISPR SPACER CONTIG MATCHES" > crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt tail -n+2 formatted_crispr_contig_hits.txt | wc -l >> crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt echo "CRISPR HITS TO ORFS" >> crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt wc -l crispr_orf_hits.txt >> crispr_orf_hits_stats.txt

To identify what the ORFs targeted by spacers encode, we blasted the ORFs against the UniProt TrEMBL database. awk '{print $2}' crispr_orf_hits.txt | sort -u | while read LINE; dogrep -A 1 $LINE ${LINE/.orf*/_orfs.fa} >> crispr_orf_hits.fadoneblastx -query crispr_orf_hits.fa -out crispr_orf_hits_blast_uniprot.txt -db /project/egricelab/references/TrEMBL/uniprot_trembl -outfmt 6 -num_threads 32 -evalue 1e-10 -max_target_seqs 1 -show_gis awk '{print $2}' crispr_orf_hits.txt | sort -u | while read LINE; dogrep -A 1 $LINE ${LINE/.orf*/_orfs.fa} >> crispr_orf_hits.fa done blastx -query crispr_orf_hits.fa -out crispr_orf_hits_blast_uniprot.txt -db /project/egricelab/references/TrEMBL/uniprot_trembl -outfmt 6 -num_threads 32 -evalue 1e-10 -max_target_seqs 1 -show_gis
